attributes.stem: A 35-year-old, G2P1, at 32 weeks gestation, presents to the local rural hospital with lower abdominal pain and clear vaginal loss. This occurred one hour ago when she was watching television. She reports normal fetal movements. Her 20 week ultrasound demonstrated a low-lying placenta and the pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following medications would be most appropriate to administer first?
attributes.explanation:
Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) refers to the spontaneous rupture of membranes prior to the 37th week of pregnancy (1-6). The lower abdominal pain and passage of clear vaginal fluid (amniotic fluid) would be consistent with this diagnosis. Prolonged PPROM (PPPROM) is the rupture of membranes greater than twenty-four hours prior to the onset of labour. The management of PPROM requires the arrangement of admission, a high vaginal swab for MC&S, a urine MC&S as well as an ultrasound to confirm cephalic presentation and determine the position of the placenta which was previously noted to be low lying at 20 weeks gestation. 

The most appropriate medication to administer first is betamethasone (2 doses of 11.4mg, IM, 24 hours apart). Corticosteroids promote fetal lung maturation and decrease the incidence of respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular haemorrhage, necrotising enterocolitis and perinatal mortality. The optimal timing of administration is between 48 hours to 7 days before delivery and can be utilised up to 35+6/40. Betamethasone is important first line treatment as it helps to increase surfactant production and decrease respiratory distress in the preterm neonate, but it takes time to have this effect- so the earlier that it is given the earlier it can start working.

It is important to delineate the type of lower abdominal pain. If the patient is contracting then nifedipine and IV benzylpenicillin are indicated. Nifedipine is not used routinely for PPROM but can be added if women are contracting as nifedipine does allow time for corticosteroid administration. Salbutamol is also not indicated as a tocolytic as it has a risk of causing maternal tachycardia as a side-effect which can then confuse the clinical picture when monitoring for chorioamnionitis.

This patient would be admitted to the ward for observation for the next 2-3 days and also commenced on erythromycin (250mg PO, QID for 10 days). The ORACLE I trial, a multicentre randomised controlled trial of 4809 females with a median gestation of thirty-two weeks, compared the use of erythromycin and augmentin DF in PPROM. Specifically, this trial found that erythromycin use decreased rates of delivery at 7 days, decreased the rate of surfactant administration and neonatal positive blood cultures as well as decreasing rates of chronic lung disease and major cerebral haemorrhage. There was no evidence of harm to the neonate with the use of this antibiotic. However, the priority must be to consider the risks of pulmonary complications and to give betamethasone.

A magnesium infusion is not recommended for this patient as she does not have preeclampsia and it is not indicated for fetal neuroprotection past thirty weeks gestation.

PPROM complicates 2-4% of singleton births and 7-20% of twin pregnancies and is associated with ascending infection from the lower vaginal tract. 50% of women will spontaneously labour within twenty-four hours and 70-90% will labour within 7 days. If PPPROM occurs without spontaneous onset of labour, an Induction of labour is recommended by 37 weeks - the timing of this will be dependent on local unit protocols.
learningPoints: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) refers to the spontaneous rupture of membranes prior to the 37th week of pregnancy.
references: Thomson, AJ, on behalf of the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Care of Women Presenting with Suspected Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes from 24+0 Weeks of Gestation.BJOG 2019; 126:e152–166
Kenyon, SL, Taylor, DJ, Tarnow-Mordi, W (2001): Broad spectrum antibiotics for preterm, prelabour rupture of fetal membranes: the ORACLE I randomised trial. THE LANCET, Vol 357, pp979-988.
Caughey, A.B, Robinson, J.N, Norwitz, E.R (2008): Contemporary Diagnosis and Management of Preterm Premature Rupture of Membranes. Rev Obstet Gynecol. 1(1):11-22.

Morris, J.M, Roberts, C.L, Buchanan, S.L, Henderson-Smart, D.J, Salkeld, G (2006): Protocol for the immediate delivery versus expectant care of women with preterm prelabour rupture of the membranes close to term (PPROMT) Trial. BMC Pregnancy and Childbirth, 6:9.
Parry, S, Strauss, JF (1998): PREMATURE RUPTURE OF THE FETAL MEMBRANES. New England Journal of Medicine Vol 338 (10) pp 663-675.

Dicker P, Untersheider J, Daly S, Geary M, Kennelly M, McAuliffe F, O’Donoghue K, Hunter A, Morrison J, Burke G (2014): Antenatal corticosteroids in IUGR: are we getting  it right? Supplement to JANUARY 2014 American Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology S65

Murphy K, Hannah M, Willan A, Hewson S, Ohlsson A, Kelly E, Matthews S, Saigal S, Asztalos, Ross S, Delisle M, Amankwah K, Guselle P, Gafni A, Lee S, Armson B (2008): Multiple courses of antenatal corticosteroids for preterm birth (MACS): a randomised controlled trial. Lancet 2008; 372: 2143–51.
